claim: The 70.1% hapax ratio is substantially artifactual: stripping word-final glyphs from the set {y,n,l,r,o} reduces unique word types by at least 40%, revealing a core vocabulary consistent with a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin or Italian
evidence_for: Hapax ratio 70.1% is anomalously high relative to natural language corpora of similar size
Word-final glyph set {y,n,l,r,o} accounts for a disproportionate share of word-type diversity
High-confidence belief (0.664) that stripping final glyphs collapses hapax count significantly
Recipes section has largest word count and second-highest entropy â€” consistent with suffix-heavy encoding
evidence_against: If suffix stripping still leaves high hapax ratio, the hypothesis fails
Truly synthetic suffixes would produce more uniform final-glyph distributions than observed
test: Strip all word-final glyphs belonging to {y,n,l,r,o} from every token, recompute vocabulary size and hapax ratio on the resulting stem corpus
test_metric: Post-stripping hapax ratio and unique stem count vs. original 8,261 unique words
test_threshold: Confirmation if post-stripping hapax ratio drops below 40%; denial if it remains above 60%

tests_run.test: strip all word-final glyphs belonging to {y,n,l,r,o} from every token, recompute vocabulary size and
tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

reasoning: If a large fraction of apparent vocabulary diversity is produced by mechanical suffix attachment, the underlying cipher table is much smaller than raw statistics suggest, dramatically narrowing the decipherment search space
